summary: Gruber dissects NBC's claim that Microsoft agreed to build copyright filtering technology into the Zune as part of a content distribution deal. He systematically examines why every proposed technical approach — fingerprinting databases, watermarking, or DRM-only playback — would be either infeasible or commercially suicidal for a device with only 4% market share. He concludes Microsoft likely promised NBC something they never intended to deliver. An update reveals Microsoft explicitly denied any such plans, vindicating Gruber's skepticism.
key_points: NBC's stated reasons for preferring Microsoft over Apple were flexible pricing and a promise to build copyright filtering into devices
Every technically feasible approach to copyright filtering — fingerprint databases, watermarking, DRM-only playback — has fatal practical flaws
A copyright filtering system must be extremely accurate in both directions: too permissive is pointless, too strict is infuriating
Microsoft with 4% market share cannot afford to hobble the Zune with restrictions that would further suppress adoption
Microsoft likely made promises to NBC it had no intention of keeping, and a subsequent Microsoft denial confirmed this
money_quotes: But so how could this possibly work? What could Microsoft do that would satisfy these demands from NBC?
Smells a bit like magic, though, to expect a 'fingerprint' system to accurately identify an episode of a particular TV series.
It's the sort of system that needs to be extremely accurate. If the matching algorithm is too permissive and fails to identify much of the material it's supposed to catch, what's the point?

If it's too strict, and incorrectly flags non-infringing video and blocks you from watching them (or, worse, 'removes' them from your video library — and note that 'remove' is indeed the verb NBC jerkos have used when describing this dystopic feature), well, that'd be infuriating, to say the least.
It'd be one thing if Microsoft were the company with 70 percent share in the handheld media player market, but they're not. They're the company with 4 percent share.
Microsoft is craven, and they've been known to engage in boil-the-ocean sorts of software projects before, but they're not stupid.
So I think they've pulled a fast one on NBC, promising them something they have no intention to deliver.
key_insight: Microsoft likely promised NBC copyright filtering technology it had no intention of building, because actually delivering it would have killed the already-marginal Zune.
